1: peri de tōn pneumatikōn (peri de tōn pneumatikōn): the phrase is grammatically ambiguous — the adjective pneumatikōn can be neuter plural ('spiritual things/gifts') or masculine plural ('spiritual persons'). The neuter reading suits the context better: Paul is addressing what spiritual gifts are and how they function. Ou thelō hymas agnoein (I do not want you to be ignorant) — a Pauline disclosure formula that signals an authoritative statement follows.
2: ethnē ete pros ta eidōla ta aphōna hōs an ēgesthe apagomenoi: 'you were Gentiles, led away to mute idols however you were led.' Aphōna (mute/voiceless) ironically contrasts with the Spirit-speech Paul is about to discuss — idols cannot speak, the Spirit does. Apagomenoi (led away) is a passive participle of compulsion: the pagan past was characterized by being driven rather than led by God's Spirit.
3: anathema Iēsous vs. kyrios Iēsous: the two confessional poles. Anathema = devoted to destruction, an LXX technical term for objects of divine curse (Deut 7:26). Whether 'Jesus be cursed' was a formula forced on believers under persecution or a Corinthian spiritual-pride claim ('the earthly Jesus is irrelevant, only the heavenly Christ matters') is debated. Either way, Paul's criterion is clear: the Spirit of God produces the confession of Jesus's lordship, never his cursing.
4: diaireseis charismaton (diaireseis charismaton): diairesis = distribution or distinction/variety — the same Spirit distributes various gifts rather than one gift to all. Charisma (grace-gift, plural charismata) is Paul's own coinage built on charis (grace) — a gift whose basis is God's favor, not human merit or capacity. The three-verse structure (vv. 4-6) attributes the variety of gifts, services, and workings to the Spirit, Lord, and God respectively — an early Trinitarian-patterned statement.
5: diaireseis diakoniōn (diaireseis diakoniōn): diakonia = service, ministry — the same word used for table-waiting (Acts 6:2) and apostolic ministry (2 Cor 6:3). Paul deliberately places 'service' as the second category of Spirit-distribution after 'gifts' — the charismata are not status-conferring trophies but service-functions within the community. Ho autos kyrios (the same Lord) — Christ is the source of the variety of ministry-forms.
6: energēmata (energēmata): workings, operations — from energeō (to be at work, cf. English 'energy'). The third category: active divine workings that express the gifts and services. Ho de autos Theos ho energōn ta panta en pasin (the same God who works all things in all people) — the sovereignty of God over all pneumatic activity: what looks like human charismatic diversity is the unified working of one God through many persons.
7: phanerōsis tou pneumatos (phanerōsis tou pneumatos): manifestation of the Spirit — phanerōō = to make visible/apparent. The individual gift is the Spirit's self-disclosure through the person; the gift is not owned by the receiver but is the Spirit appearing through them. Pros to sympheron (for the common good) — sympheron = advantage, benefit (same word as 6:12 and 10:23); the criterion for evaluating any exercise of a gift is its communal benefit.
8: logos sophias / logos gnōseōs: 'utterance of wisdom / utterance of knowledge.' Both are logos-compounds: not wisdom or knowledge as possession but as speech-act, oral communication. In the Corinthian context where sophia (wisdom) was the premium currency (1:17-2:5), Paul democratizes it: wisdom comes as a Spirit-given utterance to specific persons for specific occasions, not as the general intellectual achievement of the sophos.
9: pistis (faith, v. 9): distinct from saving faith — this is a special gift of faith for miraculous action (cf. 13:2: 'faith to move mountains'). Charismata iamatōn (gifts of healing): the plural of both charisma and iama (healing) is unusual — perhaps indicating multiple types of healing gifts or multiple instances, each a distinct grace-gift rather than a permanent endowment.
10: energēmata dynameōn (workings of powers/miracles): the dynamis vocabulary for miraculous acts; diakrisis pneumatōn (discernment of spirits): diakrisis = judgment, distinguishing — evaluating which spirits/prophecies are genuinely from God (cf. 1 John 4:1). Genē glōssōn (kinds of tongues): genē = varieties/kinds, suggesting differentiation even within the tongue-gift. Hermēneia glōssōn (interpretation of tongues): the complementary gift enabling tongues to function as intelligible speech in the assembly.
11: to hen kai to auto pneuma (the one and the same Spirit): hen and auto together emphatically assert unity — not merely one of a kind but numerically identical across all manifestations. Diairoun (distributing): the active participle of diairō = to divide and distribute — echoing diairesis in vv. 4-6. Kath' hekastō (to each one individually): the Spirit's distribution is personal and intentional, not random.
12: houtōs kai ho Christos (so also Christ): the body-analogy's conclusion identifies the body not merely 'with the church' but with Christ himself — the body is Christ. This goes beyond illustration: the Corinthians' treatment of the body's members is their treatment of Christ's own body. The Christological intensity of the ecclesiology emerges here: harming the community is harming Christ (cf. 9:5: 'whom you are persecuting').
13: en heni pneumati eis hen sōma ebaptisthēmen (in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body): the preposition en is instrumental ('by') or locative ('in') — either the Spirit is the agent or the sphere of baptism. Eis hen sōma (into one body) — baptism is not individual spiritual transaction but corporate incorporation. Eite Ioudaioi eite Hellēnes eite douloi eite eleutheroi: the social binary pairs (ethnic, legal) are transcended by the Spirit's baptism — the body is constituted across the deepest social divisions of the Roman world.
14: to sōma ouk estin hen melos alla polla (the body is not one member but many): the premise for the following argument against gift-competition. The body's plurality is not a problem to overcome but the structural reality that makes the body a body. A one-member body is not a body at all — it is a monstrosity. Paul uses this anatomical truism to attack the Corinthian impulse to reduce the body's richness to a single valued gift (tongues).
15: ean eipen ho pous (if the foot should say): the personified body-parts speak — the device allows Paul to give the gift-competition argument a reductio ad absurdum form. Para touto ouk estin ek tou sōmatos (on account of that it does not belong to the body) — the self-exclusion logic is exposed as incoherent: no body-part decides its own body-membership. The foot cannot disqualify itself by comparing itself unfavorably to the hand.
16: ean eipen to ous (if the ear should say): the second personification, repeating the structure. The parallel body-part arguments (foot/hand, ear/eye) hit different audience members: those who feel excluded (foot/ear) and those who might exclude others (eye saying to the hand, v. 21). Both are anatomical absurdities that expose the absurdity of spiritual gift competition and dismissal.
17: ei holon to sōma ophthalmos (if the whole body were an eye): the reductio from the body-pluralism argument. If the Corinthians got what they apparently wanted — everyone with the same gift (tongues being the most visible) — the 'body' would be a grotesque monstrosity, not a functioning organism. Pou hē akoe (where would the hearing be?); pou hē osphrēsis (where would the sense of smell be?) — the anatomy drives the ecclesiology.
18: nun de ho Theos etheto ta melē hekastou autōn en tō sōmati kathōs ēthelésen (God has placed each member in the body as he chose): etheto (aorist of tithēmi, to place, appoint) — God's active arrangement of the body's diversity is a creation-level decision, not an accidental variation. Kathōs ēthelésen (as he chose) — the theological grounding: gift-distribution reflects divine sovereignty and intention, not spiritual achievement.
19: ei de ēn ta panta hen melos (if all were one member): the hypothetical homogeneity argument continued. Pou to sōma (where would the body be?) — the body would cease to exist as a body. The argument proves from anatomy that unity requires diversity: a body of identical members is not a body but a mass of undifferentiated tissue. The Corinthians' preference for uniformity (all speak in tongues) would, paradoxically, destroy the very body they belong to.
20: nun de polla men melē hen de sōma (many members but one body): the summary thesis of the body-analogy argument. The men/de construction (many... but one) holds the tension without collapsing either side. This is Paul's ecclesiological statement in its most compressed form: the community is one organism precisely because it is composed of diverse, non-interchangeable members. The unity is not uniformity.
21: ou dynatai ho ophthalmos eipein tē cheiri chreia sou ouk echō (the eye cannot say to the hand 'I have no need of you'): the argument from an opposing direction — now the strong member cannot exclude the seemingly weaker one. Ou dynatai (cannot) — the impossibility is anatomical and theological. The body's interdependence is a structural feature, not a moral aspiration. The eye's apparent independence from the hand is an illusion of function-specialization, not independence of existence.
22: ta dokounte asthenesteron hyparchein melē tou sōmatos anankaia estin (the members that seem weaker are indispensable): asthenesteron = weaker, more fragile (the comparative of asthenēs); anankaia = necessary, indispensable — the paradox that the apparently fragile members are the most necessary. This overturns the Corinthian honor-scale: the visible, dramatic gifts (tongues) are not more valuable than the apparently weaker ones (helps, administration).
23: ta atimōtera tou sōmatos toutois timēn perissoteran peritheumen (on these we bestow greater honor): atimotera (less honored, comparative of atimos) vs. perissoteran timēn (greater honor) — the honor-reversal: human practice actually reinforces the body's care for less-honored members by clothing them more carefully. Euschēmosynēn perissoteran echei (has greater modesty) — the anatomy of clothing practices demonstrates the principle Paul wants for the church.
24: ta de euschēmona hēmōn ou chreian echei (our presentable members have no need): euschēmona (well-formed, honorable) — the presentable, naturally dignified members need no additional honor. Synekrasen to sōma tō hysterountī perissoterēn dous timēn (God has blended the body together, giving greater honor to the lesser part): synkeraō = to mix together, blend — God is the one who deliberately constructs the body with this compensatory honor-distribution to the disadvantaged members.
25: hina mē ē schisma en tō sōmati (so that there may be no division in the body): schisma = tear, division — the same word used for the community divisions in 1:10 and 11:18. The body-analogy's purpose is anti-schismatic: understanding the body's unity-in-diversity is the cure for factional gift-competition. Merimnosōsin (care for one another) — merimnaō = to be anxious about, to take care of; the mutual concern is the positive expression of the body's unity.
26: ei pathei hen melos sympaschei panta ta melē (if one member suffers, all the members suffer together): sympaschō = to suffer together with (syn-compound) — the involuntary solidarity of a body that shares neural connection. Syndoxazetai panta ta melē (if one member is honored, all the members rejoice together) — syndoxazō = to be glorified together. The positive and negative solidarity are symmetrical: what happens to one happens to all. This is the theological basis for communal lament and communal celebration.
27: hymeis de este sōma Christou kai melē ek merous (you are the body of Christ and individually members of it): the direct ecclesiological claim, no longer analogy. Sōma Christou (body of Christ) — the community is identified as Christ's body, not merely organized like a body. Ek merous (each in his part/individually) — each person is a member, not the whole. The corporate identification with Christ is total; the individual's position within it is partial and defined by relation to the whole.
28: etheto ho Theos en tē ekklēsia prōton apostolous deuteron prophētas triton didaskalous (God has appointed in the church first apostles, second prophets, third teachers): prōton... deuteron... triton is a ranked sequence, then the list shifts to ungrouped items. Antilēmpseis (acts of helping) — a hapax in Paul, from antilambanō = to take hold, to help; the term may suggest supporting the weak. Kyberneseis (acts of guiding/administration) — from kybernaō, to pilot/steer a ship; used for governance and organizational leadership.
29: mē pantes apostoloi (are all apostles?): the rhetorical mē expects a negative answer — 'not all are apostles, are they?' The sequence of seven rhetorical questions (vv. 29-30) demolishes any claim that one gift is universally distributed. Mē pantes charismata echōsin iamatōn (do all have gifts of healing?) — the negative answer to each question is the argument against the Corinthian elevation of any single gift as the mark of genuine spirituality.
30: mē pantes glōssais lalousin (do all speak in tongues?): the question aimed directly at the Corinthian preference. The implied answer — 'of course not' — undermines the unstated claim that tongues is the spiritual gift that all genuine believers must manifest. Mē pantes diermēneuousin (do all interpret?) — the follow-up makes the point: the gift of tongues requires the paired gift of interpretation to be communally useful.
31: zēloute de ta charismata ta meizona (eagerly desire the greater gifts): zēloō = to be zealous for, to eagerly pursue — the same verb will appear in 14:1, 39. The 'greater' gifts are those most beneficial for the community (prophecy over tongues, because prophecy is intelligible). Kai eti kath' hyperbolēn hodon hymin deiknymi (and I show you a still more excellent way) — kath' hyperbolēn = beyond all comparison, superabundantly; the transition to the love-chapter. Love is not a gift but the way in which all gifts must operate.
